CITY OF ESCONDIDO

CITY ATTORNEY'S OFFICE
CIVIC CENTER PLAZA
201 North Broadway
Escondido, California 92025-2798
(619) 741-4608
FAX (619) 741-7541

December 4, 1991


Martha F. Stanton
2091 East Valley Parkway
Escondido, California 92027

Re: Campaign Contributions

Dear Ms. Stanton:

I respond to your letter of November 26, 1991, in which you indicated some continuing concern about the proper interpretation of Escondido Municipal Code sections governing campaign contributions from a husband and wife.

The opinion memorandum issued by the office on April 14, 1986, is best summarized as standing for the proposition that the Escondido Municipal Code presumes that contributions from a husband and wife are considered to be from a single source and are cumulative. However, as noted in the memorandum, this conclusion only exists "absent unusual circumstances."

Indeed, this position has been expressed in previous advice to candidates, in which this office advised that contributions from spouses will be added together for the purpose of determining the $250.00 campaign contribution limit unless it can be determined that "contributions come from separate as opposed to community property." (Letter to Sid Hollins, dated May 8, 1990, copy attached.)

In evaluating what standards are to be used in determining whether or not campaign contributions are to be treated as from a single source, this office has consistently looked to the definition found in the Political Reform Act of 1974, the Guidelines, and the decisions of the Fair Political Practices Commission (see April 14, 1986, memorandum, page 2, second full paragraph.)

As you have noted in your November 26, 1991, letter, the FPPC has specific advice as to when contributions are to be considered from a single source, and when such contributions are to be considered from separate sources. The

COPY

Martha F. Stanton
December 4, 1991
page 2

position of the FPPC will apply in evaluations as to whether or not contributions
from a husband and wife come from a single or separate sources for purposes of
the Escondido Municipal Code campaign contribution limits.

I hope this answers your questions. If not please let me know.

Very truly yours,

JEFFREY R. EFF
Assistant City Attorney

JRE/jls

cc: David Chapman, City Attorney

COPY

Martha F. Stanton
Certified Public Accountant

2091 East Valley Parkway                                                                    Telephone (619) 746-3680
Escondido, California 92027                                                               Fax (619) 746-3613

American Institute of                                                                          California Society of
Certified Public Accountants                                                          Certified Public Accountants


November 26, 1991

Jeffrey R. Epp
Assistant City Attorney
City of Escondido
201 North Broadway
Escondido, California 92025


Regarding:    Campaign Contributions


Dear Jeff:

     Thank you for your patience in helping me to understand the ordinances governing the
campaign contributions. I have read the City Attorney's opinion dated April 14, 1986 and,
unfortunately, I am still confused. To quote:

     "With respect to the question of contributions by spouses, the Fair Political
Practices Commission has specifically addressed the issue in informational
materials it distributes to candidates for office who are subject to the
provisions of the Political Reform Act. Consistent with the views expressed in
the Lumsdon and Kauhn opinions referenced above, the Commission has
concluded that absent unusual circumstances the 'contributions from a husband
and wife are considered to be from a single source and are cumulated...'

     "Therefore, I conclude that in both of the circumstances posed in the questions
which you have presented the contributions must be added together and be
considered as from a single source for the purpose of determining whether or not
the campaign contribution limits set forth in section 2-102 has been reached."

     When I attended an FPPC workshop, we were specifically told that spouses could each
contribute the maximum from joint funds as long as both sign the check or there was an
accompanying document so instructing and signed by both parties. Also in looking in my
manual supplied by the FPPC, it is also very specific on this matter. I am enclosing a copy of
the page from the 1990 manual A. I do not have the 1991 manual; but, FPPC informed me this
part has not changed.

     Again, I apologize for being unable to grasp this concept. But as campaign treasurer for
Elmer Cameron, the campaign committee has requested clarification as we will begin collecting
contributions very soon. Unless we hear contrary from you, we will assume the city of
Escondido is following the current FPPC guidelines.

Sincerely,


Martha F. Stanton
Certified Public Accountant

Enclosure

                                                                                                                    COPY


C.     CONTRIBUTION LIMITATIONS FOR SPECIAL ELECTIONS

The contribution limitations apply separately for a special election cycle and for a special runoff cycle when a special election is held for the purpose of filling an elective office which has become vacant.

"Special election cycle" means the day on which the office becomes vacant until the day of the special election.

"Special runoff election cycle" means the day after the special election until the day of the special runoff election.

The limitations for each of these cycles are the same amounts as the limitations for a fiscal year. For example, when a candidate is being voted upon in a special election, contributors may contribute up to the stated limits to the candidate's separate committee established for the special election even if they contributed the maximum amount in the fiscal year to a committee established by the candidate for a regular election. If the candidate is also being voted upon in a special runoff election, contributors may make additional contributions up to the stated limits in the special runoff election cycle to the candidate's special election committee.

NOTE: Transfers from a candidate's or officeholder's regular campaign bank account to a campaign bank account established by the candidate or officeholder in connection with a special election are permitted as long as such a transfer does not cause the total amount contributed by any person to the special election account to exceed the contribution limitations. The same is true for transfers from a special election campaign bank account to a regular campaign bank account.

D.     AGGREGATION OF CONTRIBUTIONS

Contributions from an individual's personal funds and from any other funds which he or she in fact directs and controls must be aggregated.

Contributions made by two or more entities must be aggregated if the same person or a majority of the same persons in fact directs and controls the contributions.

In addition, contributions made by business entities in a parent-subsidiary relationship and business entities with the same controlling owner (more than 50%) must be aggregated unless the business entities act completely independently in their decisions to make contributions. A parent-subsidiary relationship exists when one business entity owns more than 50 percent of another business entity.

Individuals may make separate contributions using jointly held funds (i.e., spouses may each contribute $1,000 to a single candidate in a fiscal year). However, when a contribution is made by a check bearing the imprinted name of more than one individual, it must be attributed to the individual who signs the check unless an accompanying document indicates the amount to be attributed to each individual. If each individual signs the check, the contribution must be attributed equally to each individual unless an accompanying document indicates otherwise. Accompanying documents must be signed by each contributing individual.

E.     SOLICITATION OF CONTRIBUTIONS

The Act prohibits the solicitation of contributions in excess of the contribution limits. Though not mandatory, to avoid violation of this prohibition, candidates and committees may include the following disclaimer on solicitations:

The Political Reform Act places a limit on the amount of contributions (including loans) made in a fiscal year (July 1- June 30). This is not a request for a contribution in excess of these limits. In most cases the limit is $1,000.

-19-


0640-70

CITY OF ESCONDIDO

CIVIC CENTER PLAZA
201 North Broadway, Escondido
California 92025-2798
(619) 741-4608
FAX (619) 741-7541

May 8, 1990

Sid Hollins
Friends of Sid Hollins
1274 La Paloma Glen
Escondido, CA 92026

Subject: Campaign Contributions

Dear Sid:

A member of the public has brought to my attention a copy of an
envelope in which you solicit campaign contributions. A portion
of the envelope states "a husband and wife may contribute up to
$250 each but no individual check can exceed $250". This
statement is not incorrect, but it could lead to some confusion
in light of the requirement that contributions from spouses will
be added together for the purpose of determining the $250
campaign contribution limit unless it can be determined that
contributions come from separate as opposed to community
property.

I enclose for your review a copy of my memo of April 14, 1986
which discusses this issue in some detail.

The purpose of this letter is simply to remind you of this
restriction so that you do not inadvertently run afoul of the
City's Campaign Contribution Ordinance.

Please call either myself or Jeffrey Epp if you have any
questions.

Yours very truly,

DAVID R. CHAPMAN
City Attorney

DRC/mce

Enclosure

COPY
